entries.title: Preface
entries.relevance_rationale: Front matter adds no statistical or linear algebra preparation.
entries.relevance_skip_rationale: Preface; not part of study content.

entries.title: Notes for the instructor
entries.relevance_rationale: Instructor notes are irrelevant for student exam preparation.
entries.relevance_skip_rationale: Instructor notes; not needed for learners.

entries.title: Chapter 1. Basic Notions
entries.relevance_rationale: Core linear algebra foundations underpin multivariable statistics.
entries.relevance_skip_rationale: Chapter heading only; no standalone content.

entries.title: 1. Vector spaces
entries.relevance_rationale: Vector spaces are foundational for all advanced multivariate methods.

entries.title: 2. Linear combinations, bases.
entries.relevance_rationale: Bases and spans are essential for understanding dimensions and models.

entries.title: 3. Linear Transformations. Matrix–vector multiplication
entries.relevance_rationale: Linear maps and matrices are central to statistical theory and computation.

entries.title: 3.2. Linear transformations F**n –> F**m. Matrix–column multiplication.
4. Linear transformations as a vector space
entries.relevance_rationale: Useful abstraction, but less directly needed than core transformation basics.

entries.title: 5. Composition of linear transformations and matrix multiplication.
entries.relevance_rationale: Composition and matrix products are ubiquitous in multivariate derivations.

entries.title: 6. Invertible transformations and matrices. Isomorphisms
entries.relevance_rationale: Invertibility matters for identifiability and coordinate changes.

entries.title: 7. Subspaces.
entries.relevance_rationale: Subspaces are essential for rank, null spaces, and projections.

entries.title: 8. Application to computer graphics.
entries.relevance_rationale: Graphics applications do not support multivariable statistics preparation.
entries.relevance_skip_rationale: Computer graphics application is irrelevant here.

entries.title: Chapter 2. Systems of linear equations
entries.relevance_rationale: Solving linear systems is fundamental for estimation and inference.

entries.title: 1. Different faces of linear systems.
entries.relevance_rationale: Multiple representations help connect equations, matrices, and geometry.

entries.title: 2. Solution of a linear system. Echelon and reduced echelon forms
entries.relevance_rationale: Row reduction is essential for solving models and understanding rank.

entries.title: 2.1.1. Row operations and multiplication by elementary matrices
3. Analyzing the pivots.
entries.relevance_rationale: Pivots determine rank, independence, and solvability.

entries.title: 3.1. Corollaries about linear independence and bases. Dimension
4. Finding the inverse of A by row reduction.
entries.relevance_rationale: Matrix inversion is useful, though less central than solving systems.

entries.title: 5. Dimension. Finite-dimensional spaces.
entries.relevance_rationale: Dimension is crucial for parameter counting and subspace reasoning.

entries.title: 5.1. Completing a linearly independent system to a basis
6. General solution of a linear system.
entries.relevance_rationale: General solutions describe solution spaces in regression and constraints.

entries.title: 7. Fundamental subspaces of a matrix. Rank.
entries.relevance_rationale: Rank and fundamental subspaces are core to multivariate analysis.

entries.title: 7.2. Explanation of the computing bases in the fundamental subspaces.
7.3. The Rank Theorem. Dimensions of fundamental subspaces.
7.4. Completion of a linearly independent system to a basis
8. Representation of a linear transformation in arbitrary bases. Change of coordinates formula.
entries.relevance_rationale: Coordinate changes are important for basis-invariant statistical reasoning.

entries.title: 8.3.1. An example: change of coordinates from the standard basis
8.3.2. An example: going through the standard basis
8.4. Matrix of a transformation and change of coordinates.
8.5. Case of one basis: similar matrices
Chapter 3. Determinants
entries.relevance_rationale: Determinants matter, but less than systems, projections, and spectra.

entries.title: 1. Introduction.
entries.relevance_rationale: Introductory material is less important than determinant properties.

entries.title: 2. What properties determinant should have.
entries.relevance_rationale: Properties help understand determinant meaning and algebraic behavior.

entries.title: 3. Constructing the determinant.
entries.relevance_rationale: Construction clarifies determinant foundations, though not heavily used later.

entries.title: 3.2. Properties of determinant deduced from the basic properties.
3.3. Determinants of diagonal and triangular matrices.
3.5. Determinants of a transpose and of a product. Determinants of elementary matrices.
4. Formal definition. Existence and uniqueness of the determinant.
entries.relevance_rationale: Formal definition supports theoretical understanding of determinants.

entries.title: 5. Cofactor expansion.
entries.relevance_rationale: Cofactor expansion is useful, but computationally secondary.

entries.title: 5.1. Cofactor formula for the inverse matrix
5.2. Some applications of the cofactor formula for the inverse.
6. Minors and rank.
entries.relevance_rationale: Minors connect determinants directly to rank and singularity.

entries.title: 7. Review exercises for Chapter 3.
entries.relevance_rationale: Exercises reinforce learning but add no new concepts.

entries.title: Chapter 4. Introduction to spectral theory (eigenvalues and eigenvectors)
entries.relevance_rationale: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors are central in multivariate statistics.

entries.title: 1. Main definitions
entries.relevance_rationale: Eigenvalue definitions are essential for covariance and PCA.

entries.title: 1.3. Finding characteristic polynomial and eigenvalues of an abstract operator
2. Diagonalization.
entries.relevance_rationale: Diagonalization underlies simplifying symmetric operators and covariance matrices.

entries.title: 2.3. The case of n distinct eigenvalues
2.4. Bases of subspaces (AKA direct sums of subspaces).
Chapter 5. Inner product spaces
entries.relevance_rationale: Inner products support geometry, projections, and least squares.

entries.title: 1. Inner product in R**n and C**n. Inner product spaces.
entries.relevance_rationale: Inner products define norms, angles, and orthogonality.

entries.title: 1.1. Inner product and norm in R**n.
1.2. Inner product and norm in C**n.
2. Orthogonality. Orthogonal and orthonormal bases.
entries.relevance_rationale: Orthogonality is fundamental for projections and decompositions.

entries.title: 3. Orthogonal projection and Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization
entries.relevance_rationale: Projections and Gram-Schmidt are essential for regression and bases.

entries.title: 4. Least square solution. Formula for the orthogonal projection
entries.relevance_rationale: Least squares is a core tool in statistical modeling.

entries.title: 5. Adjoint of a linear transformation. Fundamental subspaces revisited.
entries.relevance_rationale: Adjoints and subspaces support normal equations and operator theory.

entries.title: 5.3. The ``essential'' part of a linear transformation
6. Isometries and unitary operators. Unitary and orthogonal matrices.
entries.relevance_rationale: Useful for geometry and stability, though less central than projections.

entries.title: 7. Rigid motions in R**n
entries.relevance_rationale: Rigid motions are less relevant to statistical modeling.
entries.relevance_skip_rationale: Rigid motions are not central to statistics.

entries.title: 8. Complexification and decomplexification
entries.relevance_rationale: Complex extensions are occasionally useful but not core preparation.

entries.title: 8.3. Introducing complex structure to a real space
8.3.1. An elementary way to introduce a complex structure
8.3.2. From elementary to abstract construction of complex structure
8.3.3. An abstract construction of complex structure
8.3.4. The abstract construction via the elementary one
Chapter 6. Structure of operators in inner product spaces.
entries.relevance_rationale: Operator structure supports advanced matrix methods and decompositions.
entries.relevance_skip_rationale: Chapter heading only; no standalone content.

entries.title: 1. Upper triangular (Schur) representation of an operator.
entries.relevance_rationale: Schur form is useful, but less essential than SVD and spectral theorem.

entries.title: 2. Spectral theorem for self-adjoint and normal operators.
entries.relevance_rationale: Spectral theorem is crucial for symmetric covariance matrices.

entries.title: 3. Polar and singular value decompositions.
entries.relevance_rationale: SVD is central to PCA, dimension reduction, and estimation.

entries.title: 3.2. Modulus of an operator. Singular values.
3.4. Matrix representation of the Shmidt decomposition. Singular value decomposition.
3.4.1. From singular value decomposition to the polar decomposition
4. Applications of the singular value decomposition.
entries.relevance_rationale: SVD applications directly support multivariate statistical methods.
entries.relevance_skip_rationale: SVD applications are optional after core SVD ideas.

entries.title: 4.2. Operator norm of a linear transformation
5. Structure of orthogonal matrices
entries.relevance_rationale: Orthogonal structure is useful, but secondary to SVD and spectra.

entries.title: 6. Orientation
entries.relevance_rationale: Orientation is rarely needed in multivariable statistics.
entries.relevance_skip_rationale: Orientation is peripheral for this goal.

entries.title: 6.3. Continuous transformations of bases and orientation
Chapter 7. Bilinear and quadratic forms
entries.relevance_rationale: Quadratic forms are central to covariance and optimization.
entries.relevance_skip_rationale: Chapter heading only; no standalone content.

entries.title: 1. Main definition
entries.relevance_rationale: Definitions are needed to interpret quadratic objectives and covariance.

entries.title: 2. Diagonalization of quadratic forms
entries.relevance_rationale: Diagonalization simplifies quadratic forms in statistical analysis.

entries.title: 3. Silvester's Law of Inertia
entries.relevance_rationale: Inertia helps classify quadratic forms and definiteness.

entries.title: 4. Positive definite forms. Minimax characterization of eigenvalues and the Silvester's criterion of positivity
entries.relevance_rationale: Positive definiteness is vital for covariance matrices and optimization.

entries.title: 5. Positive definite forms and inner products
entries.relevance_rationale: Connects geometry with covariance-like structures.

entries.title: Chapter 8. Dual spaces and tensors
entries.relevance_rationale: Duality and tensors appear in advanced multivariate theory.
entries.relevance_skip_rationale: Included in the advanced multilinear stage.

entries.title: 1. Dual spaces
entries.relevance_rationale: Dual spaces support linear functionals and covariance interpretations.

entries.title: 1.1. Linear functionals and the dual space. Change of coordinates in the dual space
2. Dual of an inner product space
entries.relevance_rationale: Useful for representing functionals via inner products.

entries.title: 2.2. Is an inner product space a dual to itself?
3. Adjoint (dual) transformations and transpose. Fundamental subspace revisited (once more)

entries.title: 3.1.1. Dual transformation for the case A : F**n -> F**m
3.1.2. Dual transformation in the abstract setting

entries.title: 4.2. An example: velocities (differential operators) and differential forms as vectors and linear functionals
4.3. The case of a real inner product space

5. Multilinear functions. Tensors

entries.title: 5.2.1. Lifting a multilinear function to a linear transformation on the tensor product

6.3. Change of coordinates formula for tensors

entries.relevance_rationale: Advanced spectral theory is useful but less essential than core spectral topics.

path_stages.title: Linear algebra foundations
path_stages.focus: Build the vector-space and linear-system machinery needed for multivariable statistics.
path_stages.rationale: These topics establish vector spaces, bases, linear maps, matrix algebra, solving systems, rank, dimension, and coordinate changes—the core language for statistical theory and proofs.

path_stages.title: Determinants and invertibility
path_stages.focus: Understand determinant-based criteria for invertibility and structural matrix properties.
path_stages.rationale: Determinants support invertibility tests, volume interpretation, and later results on eigenvalues, quadratic forms, and matrix structure.

path_stages.title: Eigenvalues, diagonalization, and spectral theory
path_stages.focus: Master eigenstructure and diagonalization for covariance matrices and operator analysis.
path_stages.rationale: Spectral tools are essential for covariance analysis, PCA, quadratic forms, and advanced asymptotic/statistical methods.

path_stages.title: Inner product spaces and least squares
path_stages.focus: Develop orthogonality, projections, and least-squares methods central to estimation.
path_stages.rationale: Inner products and orthogonal projections underpin regression, estimation geometry, and numerical stability in multivariate statistics.

path_stages.title: Quadratic forms, positivity, and advanced structure
path_stages.focus: Study quadratic forms, positive definiteness, and operator structure relevant to covariance and optimization.
path_stages.rationale: Positive definite forms and duality connect directly to covariance matrices, likelihood curvature, and constrained optimization.

path_stages.title: Advanced multilinear and tensor concepts
path_stages.focus: Extend linear algebra to tensors and coordinate transformations for higher-level theory.
path_stages.rationale: These topics are useful for advanced theoretical work, though less immediately necessary than core linear algebra.

components.text:
A vector space $V$ is a collection of objects, called vectors (denoted in this book by bold lowercase letters, like $\mathbf{v}$), together with two operations: addition of vectors and multiplication by a scalar, satisfying the following axioms:

Addition axioms:
1. (Commutativity) $\mathbf{v}+\mathbf{w}=\mathbf{w}+\mathbf{v}$ for all $\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}\in V$.
2. (Zero vector) There exists $\mathbf{0}\in V$ such that $\mathbf{v}+\mathbf{0}=\mathbf{v}$ for all $\mathbf{v}\in V$.
3. (Associati...
components.note: Author uses bold lowercase for vectors; axioms mirror familiar algebra rules — no rote memorization needed; zero and additive inverse are unique; scalars typically $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$.

components.text:
The space $\mathbb{R}^n$ consists of all column vectors of size $n$,

$$
v=\begin{pmatrix}v_1\\v_2\\\vdots\\v_n\end{pmatrix},
$$

whose entries are real numbers. Addition and scalar multiplication are defined entrywise, i.e.

$$
\alpha\begin{pmatrix}v_1\\v_2\\\vdots\\v_n\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}\alpha v_1\\\alpha v_2\\\vdots\\\alpha v_n\end{pmatrix},\quad
\begin{pmatrix}v_1\\v_2\\\vdots\\v_n\end{pmatrix}+\begin{pmatrix}w_1\\w_2\\\vdots\\w_n\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}v_1+w_1\\v_2+w_2\\\vdot...
components.note: Standard example: vectors as columns, operations defined entrywise; axioms follow from scalar arithmetic.

components.text: The space $\mathbb{C}^n$ also consists of columns of size $n$, but entries are complex numbers. Addition and scalar multiplication are defined exactly as for $\mathbb{R}^n$, except scalars now may be complex. In statements that hold for both real and complex cases we will write $\mathbb{F}^n$ (where $\mathbb{F}=\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$) to avoid repeating identical arguments.
components.note: Emphasizes analog between real and complex cases; use $\mathbb{F}^n$ when results hold for both fields.

components.text: The space $M_{m\times n}$ (also denoted $M_{m,n}$) of $m\times n$ matrices consists of all rectangular arrays with $m$ rows and $n$ columns. Addition and scalar multiplication are defined entrywise. If entries (and scalars) are real we have a real vector space; if entries are complex we have a complex vector space. Formally one could write $M^{\mathbb{R}}_{m,n}$ or $M^{\mathbb{C}}_{m,n}$, but usually the field is left implicit unless it matters.
components.note: Matrices are entrywise-vector spaces; the field (real or complex) is specified only when essential.

components.text: The axioms of a vector space are the familiar algebraic rules for (real or complex) numbers. Thus the set $\mathbb{R}$ is a real vector space and $\mathbb{C}$ is a complex vector space. For the above examples all operations are performed entrywise, so the vector-space axioms hold automatically because they hold for scalars. Consequently, one often does not need to check the axioms explicitly for these constructions.
components.note: Author points out why entrywise definitions make checking axioms unnecessary; scalars themselves give basic examples.

components.text:
The space $P_n$ of polynomials of degree at most $n$ consists of all polynomials $p$ of the form

$p(t)=a_0+a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + \dots + a_n t^n$, 

where $t$ is the independent variable. Some or all coefficients $a_k$ may be $0$. With real coefficients $P_n$ is a real vector space; with complex coefficients it is a complex vector space.
components.note: Author notes coefficients play the role of vector entries; real/complex cases treated uniformly unless specified.

components.text: What are the zero vectors in each of the above examples?
components.note: Prompt encourages identifying the additive identity in each previously given vector-space example (e.g. zero polynomial, zero matrix, zero vector).

components.text: An $m\times n$ matrix is a rectangular array with $m$ rows and $n$ columns. Elements of the array are called entries of the matrix. It is convenient to denote matrix entries by indexed letters: the first index denotes the row number and the second the column number. Thus an entry can be written as $A_{j,k}$ or $(A)_{j,k}$.
components.note: Basic notation and indexing convention for matrix entries; author emphasizes row-first, column-second indexing.

components.text:
Given a matrix $A$, its transpose (denoted $A^{T}$) is obtained by transforming the rows of $A$ into the columns. Formally,

$(A^{T})_{j,k}=(A)_{k,j}$,

meaning the entry of $A^{T}$ in row $j$ and column $k$ equals the entry of $A$ in row $k$ and column $j$.
components.note: Author: transpose swaps rows and columns; later linked to adjoint of linear transformations.

components.text:
For example,

$\displaystyle\begin{pmatrix}1 & 2 & 3\\4 & 5 & 6\end{pmatrix}^{T}=\begin{pmatrix}1 & 4\\2 & 5\\3 & 6\end{pmatrix}$.

Thus the columns of $A^{T}$ are the rows of $A$, and vice versa.
components.note: Illustrates the row↔column swap; author remarks transposition will be used formally now and related to adjoint later.

components.text: The transpose of a matrix gives the adjoint transformation in linear transformations; here transposition is treated as a formal operation. A column vector $x\in\mathbb{F}^n$ (where $\mathbb{F}=\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$) can be written $x=(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_n)^T$. Displaying it vertically uses more space.
components.note: Author notes transpose relates to adjoint; here used as convenient notation for column vectors.

components.description: Question 1.2 — Which sets are vector spaces?
components.note: Author expects checking vector-space axioms (closure under addition/scalars) for each example.
components.preview: Question 1.2 — Which sets are vector spaces?

components.note: These test basic axioms and degree behavior; watch counterexamples for (4).

components.text: Prove that the zero vector $0$ of a vector space $V$ is unique.
components.note: Standard proof: assume two zeros and use additive identity property to show equality.

components.text: What matrix is the zero vector of the space $M_{2\times3}$?
components.note: Expect the $2\times3$ matrix with all entries $0$.

components.text: Prove that the additive inverse (Axiom 4) of any vector is unique.
components.note: Proof uses adding the two purported inverses and the identity to show they coincide.

components.text: Prove that $0\,v=0$ for any vector $v\in V$ (where the left $0$ is the scalar zero and the right $0$ is the zero vector).
components.note: Use distributivity and properties of additive identity in the field and vector space.

components.text: Prove that for any vector $v$ its additive inverse $-v$ is given by $(-1)v$.
components.note: Use scalar multiplication properties and uniqueness of additive inverse.
